areaTag.zh: 家事法 · 子女 · 国际搬迁

one.zh: 国际搬迁争议：法院判两名子女（2009、2013 年出生）留澳随母，父母平等共同亲职责任。
one.en: International relocation: the court let the two children (born 2009, 2013) remain in Australia with the mother; equal shared parental responsibility.

issue.zh: 在父母即将分居两国的情况下，两名青春期早期的子女随母留澳、还是随父迁往新西兰，何者符合其最佳利益？同时，法院是否应作出对某一方家长具有「强制搬迁」效果的命令？
issue.en: With the parents about to live in two countries, is it in the two early-adolescent children's best interests to remain in Australia with the mother, or relocate to New Zealand with the father? And should the court make an order with a 'coercive' effect of forcing one parent to relocate?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – Children – Best interests – Child's wishes – International relocation – Where both children were born during periods the family lived in New Zealand – Where the father seeks to return to New Zealand and have the children live with him there.

subseq.zh: 本资料库未见上诉或后续判决记录。
subseq.en: No appeal or later decision on record in this library.

html.zh:
2000 年，一对年轻情侣在新西兰开始同居——那年父亲 23 岁、母亲 21 岁，最初与父亲的父母同住。2003 年两人结婚，曾短暂迁往英国，2007 年回到澳大利亚。一个日后很关键的事实：澳大利亚是母亲的出生国，新西兰则是父亲的出生国。

2009 年长子 X 出生；不到一岁时全家于 2010 年迁往新西兰 City C 一带。2013 年次子 Y 在新西兰出生。2016 年一家又搬回澳大利亚 Region B——此时父亲已与 Ms F 有了某种关系。2017 年父亲独自回到新西兰（住在 City G），全家再度分隔；母亲体谅孩子需要父亲，约两个月后也带着两个儿子回到新西兰。

2018 年初婚姻终结（双方于 2018-01-04 分居）。同月双方签下育养协议（2018-01-31）：两个男孩由母亲在澳大利亚日常照顾；协议第 2 条明确写明双方一致认为孩子「回到澳大利亚与母亲一起生活符合其最佳利益」，并承诺即使相隔两国也尽力共同育养。此后双方基本顺利遵守。

母亲带男孩回澳后，到 2019 年初父亲也回到 Region B 之间近一年里，父子约见面四次。2019 年初母亲与 Mr H（46 岁，有两个孩子 J、K 在 Region B 一周轮换）交往；父亲则在新西兰与 Ms F（有两个孩子 Q 18 岁、R 14 岁）重组家庭。2021 年 6 月 4 日，母亲提起诉讼，请求判令孩子与她在澳生活，并申请机场监控令——反映她担心父亲会要求孩子去新西兰。

真正的导火索：父亲决定 2024 年初回新西兰 City C 任教职，去重新连接家人、祖国与 Ms F，并希望两个儿子随他同往。母亲要留在已扎根多年的澳大利亚。两国之间只能选一个。因父亲即将赴任，本案需要尽早开庭。

一份关键证据是家庭报告：资深社工 Mr N 于 2023 年 9 月 11 日出具报告。其结论对母亲（乃至双方）都「相当难以面对」——Mr N 认为，若孩子搬去新西兰，他们面临更大的动荡与不稳定风险，因此建议：如果父亲搬去新西兰，孩子应留在澳大利亚由母亲主要照顾。

孩子们的心声是全案最揪心处。法院认定，较年长的 X 真诚而强烈地希望随父去新西兰——他强烈认同自己是新西兰人，多次称那里才是他的「家」。母亲一度难以接受这是 X 真实的意愿，怀疑受了父亲影响；但法院不认同这种怀疑，认定意愿真实。耐人寻味的是，X 几乎无法与母亲谈这个话题——他清楚母亲立场与自己相反，连开口都难。法院也特别看重：若不遵从 X 的强烈意愿，会如何影响他、以及他与弟弟的关系。

次子 Y 性格不同。通过 Mr N，他表达的感受是：若住新西兰，他对母亲的思念会大于他若住澳大利亚时对父亲的思念——他与母亲此阶段的关系似乎更近。这种差别虽不大、也不具决定性，却反映了照料史、年龄与母亲对 Y 承担的育养角色。整体上 Y 更灵活，凭成熟度更能适应任何结果。

两位新伴侣都给法院留下好印象。两个男孩都对 Ms F 感到自在，而她并未试图扮演「替代母亲」——尽管一旦孩子主要在新西兰生活，她事实上将成为家中主要的日常女性角色。Mr H 同样被视为支持性的伴侣。法院认为，问题不在两位伴侣的人品。

真正的变量是那个「未经检验」（untested）的新西兰之家。法院指出，父亲因急于回乡，某种程度上低估了孩子重新适应新西兰的难度——孩子虽在新西兰住过，但始终是与父母一起，从未经历过「一个家长在澳大利亚、另一个在新西兰」的局面。若迁居，孩子要与父亲、Ms F 及其两个孩子组成新家庭，需磨合一系列未经检验的张力；与此同时父亲还要适应新工作的压力，以及第一次与 Ms F 日复一日地共同生活（此前两家每年相处不过四五周）。法院相信两人彼此的承诺，但成年人之间的承诺能否在更多压力、孩子在场时复制到整个家庭，仍属未知。

其余因素几乎拉不开差距：孩子与父母双方、双方伴侣及大家庭关系都紧密；近六年来父母维持共同照顾、共同决策（除围绕 Dr P 为 Y 安排咨询的一次分歧）；两人收入都足以分担澳新往返旅费，也都重视电子联系；尽管有人质疑父亲日后的经济投入，法院认为双方无论孩子住哪都有能力供养；不存在家庭暴力令；双方育养风格相近，都看重勤奋、教育、待人与尊重。就连首要考量也指向平衡：与父母双方维持有意义关系对孩子有益（只是分居两国后实现方式不同），而家暴、虐待、忽视等伤害风险「并不突出」。

母亲曾提议：先维持现状，12 个月后再回来重新审视这同一个问题。法院不采纳——这些孩子多年来一直活在悬而未决的不确定中，他们需要的是确定性；当下作出决定，最有助于减少日后再起诉讼。

还有一层顾虑：母亲表示，若孩子获准搬去新西兰，她「会考虑、且很可能」也随迁——但那意味着结束与 Mr H 的长期关系、卖掉已购房屋、在连接较弱的国家重新就业、失去在 Region B 的家庭支持。法院据 Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) 强调：不应作出对家长有强制效果的命令；若孩子去新西兰，母亲最终是否随迁，并不确定。

法官坦言这是一宗「极其势均力敌」的案件——无论留澳还是赴新西兰，孩子大概最终都能适应。最后他靠两点落槌：① 较年长 X 强烈意愿的分量，及若不遵从对他与弟弟关系的影响；② 迁往新西兰那个全新、未经检验之家的高度不确定。最终判定：两个孩子留在澳大利亚随母亲生活；父母对重大长期事项保持平等共同亲职责任；撤销此前所有相关命令。

法官没有忽略 X 的失望、悲伤甚至愤怒。他安排独立人士 Dr S（从事非汇报性咨询）由双方共同出资，用孩子能理解的语言解释法院为何如此裁决——即便不能让他们认同，至少帮助他们理解与释怀。母亲要承接这份「余波」；法官也直言：若父亲能在孩子面前收敛自己的失望，对 X 的平复会更有帮助——尽管对庭上数度动情的父亲而言，这并不容易。
html.en:
In 2000 a young couple began living together in New Zealand — the father was 23, the mother 21, first with the father's parents. They married in 2003, briefly moved to the UK, and returned to Australia in 2007. One detail later mattered: Australia is the mother's birth country, while New Zealand is the father's.

X, the elder son, was born in 2009; before his first birthday the family moved to the City C area of New Zealand in 2010. Y was born in New Zealand in 2013. In 2016 the family moved back to Region B in Australia — by then the father had formed a relationship of some kind with Ms F. In 2017 he returned to New Zealand alone (City G); understanding the children's need for their father, the mother followed about two months later with the two boys.

By early 2018 the marriage had ended (separation on 4 January 2018). That month they signed a parenting agreement (31 January 2018): the boys would be in the mother's day-to-day care in Australia; clause 2 expressly recorded that the parties agreed the children were returning to live in Australia with the mother because it was in their best interests, and they committed to co-parent across the distance. It was largely complied with thereafter.

After the mother returned to Australia with the boys, in the roughly year until the father also moved back to Region B in early 2019, father and children spent time on about four occasions. In early 2019 the mother began a relationship with Mr H (46, with two children, J and K, living week-about in Region B); the father re-partnered in New Zealand with Ms F (with two children, Q, 18, and R, 14). On 4 June 2021 the mother commenced proceedings seeking that the children live with her in Australia, plus an airport watch order — reflecting her concern the father would seek to take the children to New Zealand.

The trigger: the father decided to return to City C in New Zealand in early 2024 for a teaching post — reconnecting with his family, his country and Ms F — and wanted both sons with him. The mother wanted to stay in the Australia she had long called home. Only one country could be chosen. Because the father was about to start work, the matter needed an early trial.

A key piece of evidence was the family report: experienced social worker Mr N reported on 11 September 2023. His conclusion was confronting for the mother (and likely both parties) — Mr N considered the children at greater risk of upheaval and instability if they relocated to New Zealand, and recommended that if the father relocates to New Zealand, the children remain in Australia in the mother's primary care.

The children's views were the most poignant part. The court found the elder son X genuinely and strongly wished to move to New Zealand with his father — he strongly identifies as a New Zealander and has repeatedly called it his home. The mother struggled to accept the wish was truly X's, suspecting the father's influence; the court rejected that suspicion and found the wish genuine. Tellingly, X could barely discuss it with his mother — knowing her position was opposed, he found it hard even to raise. The court gave real weight to the effect on X, and on his bond with his brother, if his strong wishes were not followed.

Y, the younger son, has a different personality. Through Mr N he conveyed that he would likely miss his mother more if he lived in New Zealand than he would miss his father if he lived in Australia — his bond with his mother seems closer at this stage. The difference, though narrow and not determinative, reflects the history of care, his age and the mother's parenting role with Y. Overall Y was more flexible and, by his maturity, better able to cope with any outcome.

Both new partners impressed the court. Both boys felt comfortable with Ms F, who had not tried to present herself as an alternate mother — though if the children lived primarily in New Zealand she would, in effect, become the predominant day-to-day female role model in the home. Mr H was likewise seen as a supportive partner. The issue, the court felt, was not the character of either partner.

The real variable was the untested New Zealand household. The court noted the father, eager to return home, had to some degree minimised the difficulty of the children re-adjusting to New Zealand — they had lived there, but always with both parents, never with one parent in Australia. If they moved, they would form a new family with the father, Ms F and her two children, with untested tensions to work through; meanwhile the father would also be adjusting to a new job's stresses and, for the first time, living day-to-day with Ms F (the two families had spent no more than four or five weeks together each year). The court accepted the father and Ms F were committed to each other, but whether an adult commitment would translate to the whole household under added stress, with the children present, remained untested.

Little else separated the options: the children have strong bonds with both parents, both partners and extended family; for nearly six years the parents had shared care and joint decision-making (bar one dispute over engaging Dr P for Y's counselling); both could afford travel between Australia and New Zealand and valued electronic contact; although the father's future financial commitment was questioned, the court found both able to support the children wherever they lived; there were no family violence orders; the parents parent similarly, valuing hard work, education, getting on with others and respect. Even the primary considerations pointed to balance: a meaningful relationship with each parent benefits the children (met differently once in separate countries), while harm risks do not loom large.

The mother proposed keeping things as they were and revisiting the same question in 12 months. The court declined — these children had lived for years under this hanging uncertainty; what they needed was certainty, and deciding now was the best way to reduce the prospect of further litigation.

There was a further concern: the mother said that if the children were allowed to move to New Zealand she would consider and likely would follow — but that would mean ending her long relationship with Mr H, selling the home she had bought, finding work in a country where her connection is weaker, and losing her family support in Region B. Citing Sampson & Hartnett (No 10), the court stressed it should not make an order with a coercive effect on a parent; if the children went to New Zealand, whether the mother would ultimately follow was uncertain.

The judge called it a finely balanced case — the boys would probably adapt either way. In the end he decided on two points: ① the weight of the elder son X's strong wishes, and the effect on his bond with his brother if overridden; ② the high uncertainty of moving into a new, untested New Zealand household. The decision: the two children remain in Australia with the mother; the parents keep equal shared parental responsibility for major long-term issues; all previous related orders are discharged.

The judge did not overlook X's disappointment, sadness, even anger. He arranged for an independent person, Dr S (engaged in non-reportable counselling), jointly funded by the parties, to explain to the children in terms they could understand why the court decided as it did — so that even if they could not accept it, they might appreciate it. The mother would have to manage the fallout; and the judge said plainly it would help X if the father's own disappointment were, at least, camouflaged from the children — not easy for a father who became emotional in the witness box.

t.zh: 判决路径 · 案件走向

html.zh:
起点：子女最佳利益 ›首要（paramount）考量
↓
① 首要考量 s 60CC(2) ›有意义关系：有益　·　伤害风险：不突出
↓
② 附加考量 s 60CC(3) ›子女意愿：X 想去新西兰　·　Y 灵活
↓
★ 决定性权衡X 的意愿　⚖　迁居的不确定性
↓
③ 原则把关 ›不作出强制家长的命令
↓
结论：子女留澳随母＋ 平等共同亲职责任

此图展示法官的判断顺序，也反映这类（搬迁／养育）案件的一般走向：先确立「最佳利益」为纲，依次审查首要、附加考量（含子女意愿），再权衡决定性因素，最后受「不强制家长」等原则约束得出结论。

步骤详解 · 各步的依据与认定

总纲 · 子女最佳利益（适用原则）

法院以子女最佳利益为首要（paramount）考量；须顾及 s 60B 的目标与子女维持有意义关系的权利、s 60CC 的各项考量，并避免家暴／虐待／忽视的不可接受风险；依 s 61DA(1) 推定父母平等共同亲职责任（仅关乎重大长期决策）；同时遵守「不作出对家长有强制效果之命令」的原则（Sampson & Hartnett）。

① 首要考量 s 60CC(2)

(a) 有意义关系：对子女有益，但父母分居两国后，实现关系的方式将与现在大不相同。
(b) 伤害风险：家暴／虐待／忽视等问题在本案「并不突出」。

“these issues do not loom large in this case.”
② 附加考量 s 60CC(3) · 子女意愿

X（较年长）：真诚而强烈地希望随父去新西兰——很大程度因其强烈认同新西兰；该意愿须被赋予分量。
Y（较年幼）：经家庭报告员 Mr N，态度更灵活；离开母亲日常照顾对他是损失，但凭其成熟度更易适应。

“X genuinely expresses a desire to live with the father in New Zealand … Y is more flexible.”
★ 决定性权衡

两种安排孩子都可能适应，法院最终靠两点区分：① X 的意愿及不遵从对其与弟弟关系的影响；② 迁往新西兰的高度不确定（全新家庭、新学校、未经检验的环境）。

③ 原则把关 · 不强制家长

依 Sampson & Hartnett (No 10)，法院应避免作出对家长有强制效果的命令——父亲已知孩子未必获准同往。

结论

子女留澳随母 ＋ 父母平等共同亲职责任。
html.en:
Start: best interests ›the paramount consideration
↓
① Primary s 60CC(2) ›meaningful relationship: yes · harm: not prominent
↓
② Additional s 60CC(3) ›views: X wants NZ · Y flexible
↓
★ Decisive weighingX's wishes ⚖ uncertainty of moving
↓
③ Principle check ›no order coercing a parent
↓
Conclusion: stay in Australia with mother+ equal shared parental responsibility

This shows the judge's order of reasoning and the general path of relocation/parenting cases: set best interests as the frame, work through primary then additional considerations (incl. the child's views), weigh the decisive factors, and conclude bounded by principles such as 'no coercion of a parent'.

Step details · the basis of each step

Frame · best interests (principles)

The child's best interests are paramount; the court weighs the objects of s 60B and the right to a meaningful relationship, the s 60CC considerations, and avoids unacceptable risk of family violence/abuse/neglect; presumes equal shared parental responsibility under s 61DA(1) (major long-term decisions only); and follows the principle of not making an order with a coercive effect on a parent (Sampson & Hartnett).

① Primary considerations s 60CC(2)

(a) Meaningful relationship: beneficial to the children, but met very differently once the parents live in separate countries.
(b) Protection from harm: violence/abuse/neglect “do not loom large” here.

“these issues do not loom large in this case.”
② Additional considerations s 60CC(3) · children's views

X (elder): genuinely and strongly wishes to move to New Zealand with his father — largely because he strongly identifies with NZ; the wish must be given weight.
Y (younger): via the family report writer Mr N, more flexible; losing his mother's day-to-day care would be a loss, but he would cope more easily.

“X genuinely expresses a desire to live with the father in New Zealand … Y is more flexible.”
★ Decisive weighing

Both arrangements would likely work; the court distinguished them on two points: ① X's wishes and the effect on his bond with his brother if overridden; ② the high uncertainty of moving to NZ (new family, new schools, untested environment).

③ Principle check · no coercion

Per Sampson & Hartnett (No 10), avoid an order with a coercive effect on a parent — the father chose to move knowing the children might not be permitted to go.

Conclusion

Children remain in Australia with the mother + equal shared parental responsibility.

⚠ 时效补充
本案适用 2024-05-06 改革前的法律。自该日起（Family Law Amendment Act 2023）：s 61DA 平等共同亲职责任推定已废除（由 s 61DAA／61DAB 取代）；s 60CC 最佳利益因素被重写（取消首要／附加层级，新增 s 60CC(2A) 安全考量）；s 60B 目标被简化。点击上方各法条可查看条文与现行状态。

⚠ Currency supplement
This case applies the law before 6 May 2024. From then (Family Law Amendment Act 2023): the s 61DA presumption of equal shared parental responsibility was repealed (replaced by s 61DAA/61DAB); s 60CC best-interests factors were rewritten (primary/additional hierarchy removed; new s 60CC(2A) on safety); the s 60B objects were simplified. Tap each provision above for its content and current status.

html.zh:
第 1 步 · 定性
“This is, as everyone has described, a finely balanced matter. Probably, the boys will cope … in time.”
✎ CasePocket 解读

法院坦言这是「极其势均力敌」的案件——两种安排孩子最终都可能适应。正因如此，结论取决于少数关键差异因素。

第 2 步 · 两个决定性因素
“(a) what weight can be applied to X’s wishes … and (b) the uncertainties of these children … moving to New Zealand … new schools; new dynamics … quite untested.”
✎ CasePocket 解读

压向澳洲的是两点：① X 的意愿及不遵从对其与弟弟关系的影响；② 迁往新西兰意味着全新家庭、新学校、未经检验的环境，不确定性高。

第 3 步 · 不强制家长（援引判例）
“Authorities such as Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) … the court should be careful in making orders which have a coercive effect upon a parent.”
✎ CasePocket 举例 · 理解「强制效果」

若判孩子「必须」随父去新西兰，等于把母亲也逼到墙角：要么放弃在澳生活随迁，要么与孩子分离。法律谨慎对待这种通过「子女住哪」间接强制家长人生抉择的效果。（本例为 CasePocket 解读，非判决原文。）

第 4 步 · 结论与善后
“For the Reasons expressed, I have formed the view that the children should remain living in Australia with the mother.”
✎ CasePocket 解读

结论：子女留澳随母。法院亦安排独立人士 Dr S 以孩子能懂的语言解释裁决原因，以缓解（尤其 X 的）失望。
html.en:
Step 1 · framing
“This is, as everyone has described, a finely balanced matter. Probably, the boys will cope … in time.”
✎ CasePocket explainer

The court calls it "finely balanced" — the boys would likely adapt either way. So the outcome turns on a few decisive differences.

Step 2 · two decisive factors
“(a) what weight can be applied to X’s wishes … and (b) the uncertainties of these children … moving to New Zealand … new schools; new dynamics … quite untested.”
✎ CasePocket explainer

Two things tip it towards Australia: ① X's wishes and the effect on his bond with his brother if overridden; ② moving to NZ means a new family, new schools, an untested environment — high uncertainty.

Step 3 · no coercion of a parent (authority)
“Authorities such as Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) … the court should be careful in making orders which have a coercive effect upon a parent.”
✎ CasePocket example · "coercive effect"

Ordering the children to move with the father would corner the mother: relocate too, or be separated from the children. The law is cautious about indirectly forcing a parent's life choice through "where the children live". (This example is CasePocket's, not the judgment.)

Step 4 · conclusion & aftercare
“For the Reasons expressed, I have formed the view that the children should remain living in Australia with the mother.”
✎ CasePocket explainer

Conclusion: the children stay in Australia with the mother. The court also arranged for an independent person, Dr S, to explain the decision to the children in terms they can understand, easing X's disappointment.

t.zh: 本案确立什么 · 可援引于
t.en: What this case establishes · cite for

html.zh:
程序背景
FCFCOA Division 1 初审（first instance）终局命令；对其他法院具说服力、非严格约束力；上诉途径为 FedCFamC1A。
判决理由 Ratio（裁决核心）
搬迁/养育争议以子女最佳利益为首要考量。当两种安排都「可行」、案情势均力敌时，法院可凭少数决定性因素区分——本案为：① 较年长子女真诚而强烈的意愿（及其对手足关系的影响）；② 迁入一个「未经检验」之家的高度不确定。同时，法院应避免作出对家长有强制（coercive）效果的命令。
附带意见 Obiter（参考性）
① 平等共同亲职责任命令在新西兰的可执行性（无本国法证据、凭双方行为可实现）；② 拒绝「12 个月后再议」、强调确定性以减少后续诉讼；③ 家庭报告（Mr N）建议的分量——均属个案性观察。
与判例的关系
援引并适用 Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) 关于「不强制家长」的原则；整体延续 s 60CC 最佳利益框架（旧版）。
可援引于 Cite for
① 搬迁案「最佳利益 + 决定性因素」的权衡方法；② 对具强制效果命令的审慎；③ 成熟子女强烈意愿的分量；④ 以「确定性」反对推迟裁决。
实务要点 Practice
即使多数因素中性，一名子女基于身份认同的强烈意愿也可能成为决定性；「未经检验的新家庭」可作区分因素；作出任何强制搬迁命令前，须审查其合理可行性。
★ 学生考点
标准的「relocation 依最佳利益判定 + 不强制家长」范例；务必注意本案适用旧版 s 60CC（见法条板块的时效说明）。
适用边界
初审、事实高度个案化；适用 2024-05-06 改革前的法律——援引时须结合现行法。
html.en:
Procedural posture
FCFCOA Division 1 first-instance final orders; persuasive, not strictly binding; appeal lies to FedCFamC1A.
Ratio (the core)
Relocation/parenting disputes turn on the child's best interests. Where both arrangements are workable and the case is finely balanced, the court may distinguish them on a few decisive factors — here: the elder child's genuine, strong wishes (and the effect on the sibling bond); and the high uncertainty of moving into an "untested" household. The court should also avoid orders with a coercive effect on a parent.
Obiter
Enforceability in NZ of an equal-shared-parental-responsibility order; rejecting a "review in 12 months" in favour of certainty; and the weight of the family report (Mr N) recommendation — case-specific observations.
Relationship to authority
Applies Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) on not coercing a parent; consistent with the (old) s 60CC best-interests framework.
Cite for
the "best interests + decisive factors" weighing in relocation; caution about coercive orders; the weight of a mature child's strong wishes; certainty over deferral.
Practice points
Even where most factors are neutral, one child's identity-based strong wishes can be decisive; an "untested new household" can discriminate; consider reasonable practicability before any coercive relocation order.
★ Exam point
A model "relocation on best interests + no coercion" case; note it applies the old s 60CC (see the Currency note in Legislation).
Limits
First-instance, highly fact-specific; applies pre-6 May 2024 law — combine with current law when relying on it.

html.zh:
Best interests of the child · 子女最佳利益
判定一切子女事项的首要（paramount）标准。
Paramount consideration · 首要考量
高于其他一切、必须最优先衡量的考量。
Parental responsibility · 亲职责任
就子女重大长期事项作决定的责任（与相处时间无关）。
Equal shared parental responsibility · 平等共同亲职责任
父母平等共担重大长期决策；旧法有此推定，2024-05-06 起推定已废除。
Relocation · 搬迁
一方拟带子女迁居（含跨国），须经法院按最佳利益衡量。
Coercive effect · 强制效果
命令实际上「逼迫」家长作出某种人生选择（如被迫随迁）。
Family report · 家庭报告
由家庭顾问/社工（本案 Mr N）就子女安排提供的专业评估与建议。
Airport watch order · 机场监控令
防止子女被擅自带离澳大利亚的命令。
Interim vs final order · 临时令 / 终局令
临时令在诉讼进行中暂时适用；终局令终结争议。
First instance · 初审
案件首次审理（非上诉）；初审判决具说服力而非约束力。
Leave to appeal · 上诉许可
就某些（如临时性）裁定上诉前需先取得法院许可。
Catchwords · 判例提要
判决开头的主题词，概括争点与领域，便于检索。
Ratio decidendi · 判决理由
判决据以成立的核心法律理由，具先例意义。
Obiter dictum · 附带意见
非裁决所必需的附带评述，仅具参考价值。
De-identified / pseudonym · 去识别化 / 化名
家事判决以化名发布以保护当事人身份（Part XIVB）。
Disclosure obligation · 披露义务
当事人须充分、坦诚披露财务等信息；隐瞒损害司法。
html.en:
Best interests of the child
The paramount standard for all decisions about children.
Paramount consideration
The consideration that outweighs all others and comes first.
Parental responsibility
Responsibility for major long-term decisions (separate from time spent).
Equal shared parental responsibility
Parents share major long-term decision-making equally; the old presumption was repealed on 6 May 2024.
Relocation
A parent proposing to move the children (incl. overseas); weighed on best interests.
Coercive effect
An order that in effect forces a parent's life choice (e.g. to relocate).
Family report
An expert assessment and recommendation on children's arrangements, by a family consultant/social worker (here Mr N).
Airport watch order
An order to prevent a child being removed from Australia without authority.
Interim vs final order
An interim order applies while proceedings continue; a final order ends the dispute.
First instance
The first hearing (not an appeal); persuasive, not binding, on other courts.
Leave to appeal
Permission needed before appealing certain (e.g. interlocutory) decisions.
Catchwords
Topic keywords at the start of a judgment summarising issues and field.
Ratio decidendi
The core legal reason the decision rests on; carries precedent.
Obiter dictum
A by-the-way remark, not essential to the decision; persuasive only.
De-identified / pseudonym
Family-law judgments are published under pseudonyms to protect identities (Part XIVB).
Disclosure obligation
Parties must give full and frank disclosure; concealment undermines justice.

desc.zh: Part VII 的立法目标；本案援引其目标与「有意义关系」。
desc.en: The objects of Part VII; the case drew on them and the 'meaningful relationship' idea.

oldact.zh:
60B 本部分的目标及其基本原则
(1) 本部分的目标是通过以下方式确保子女最佳利益得到实现：
(a) 在符合子女最佳利益的最大范围内，确保子女享有父母双方对其生活的有意义参与；
(b) 保护子女免受来自虐待、忽视或家庭暴力的身体或心理伤害；
(c) 确保子女获得充分而恰当的养育；
(d) 确保父母履行对子女照料、福祉与发展的义务与责任。
(2) 基本原则（除非违背子女最佳利益）包括：子女有权认识父母双方并由其照料；有权定期与父母双方及对其重要之人相处与沟通；父母共同分担义务与责任。
oldact.en:
60B Objects of Part and principles underlying it
(1) The objects are to ensure children's best interests are met by:
(a) ensuring children have the benefit of both parents having a meaningful involvement in their lives, to the maximum extent consistent with best interests; and
(b) protecting children from physical or psychological harm from abuse, neglect or family violence; and
(c) ensuring children receive adequate and proper parenting; and
(d) ensuring parents fulfil their duties and responsibilities.
(2) Underlying principles include: children's right to know and be cared for by both parents; to spend time and communicate regularly with both parents and others significant to them; and parents jointly sharing duties.

act.zh:
60B 本部分的目标
本部分的目标是：
(a) 确保子女的最佳利益得到实现，包括确保其安全；以及
(b) 落实 1989 年 11 月 20 日于纽约订立的《儿童权利公约》。
act.en:
60B Objects of Part
The objects of this Part are:
(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and
(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child (New York, 20 Nov 1989).

src.en: From the judgment: “the objects of s 60B … meaningful relationship …”

t.zh: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) — s 60CC（首要考量）

desc.zh: 本案据 s 60CC(2) 首要考量分析：(a) 有意义关系、(b) 保护免受伤害（且对 (b) 给予更大权重）。
desc.en: The case analysed the s 60CC(2) primary considerations: (a) meaningful relationship; (b) protection from harm (greater weight to (b)).

oldact.zh:
60CC 法院如何判定何为子女最佳利益
(1) 法院须考量第 (2)、(3) 款列出的事项。
(2) 首要考量（primary）：
(a) 子女与父母双方维持有意义关系对子女的益处；
(b) 保护子女免受来自虐待、忽视或家庭暴力的身体或心理伤害的需要。
(2A) 适用第 (2) 款时，法院须对第 (2)(b) 项（保护免受伤害）给予更大权重。
(3) 附加考量（additional）包括：
(a) 子女表达的任何意愿（及其成熟度／理解力等因素）；
(b) 子女与父母各方及他人（含祖父母等亲属）关系的性质；
(c) 父母各自参与子女重大长期事项决策、与子女相处及沟通的程度；
(ca) 父母各自履行抚养义务的程度；
(d) 子女处境若改变（含与父母任一方或其他重要之人分离）的可能影响；
……及其他因素 (e)–(m)。

act.zh:
60CC 法院如何判定何为子女最佳利益
(2) 一般考量——法院须考量：
(a) 何种安排能促进子女及其每位照料者的安全（含免受家庭暴力、虐待、忽视或其他伤害）；
(b) 子女表达的任何意愿；
(c) 子女在发展、心理、情感及文化方面的需要；
(d) 拥有或拟拥有亲职责任之人满足上述需要的能力；
(e) 在安全前提下，子女与父母及对其重要之人保持关系的益处；
(f) 与该子女具体情况相关的其他事项。
(2A) 还须考量任何家庭暴力、虐待或忽视的历史及任何家庭暴力令。
(3) 附加考量——子女享有其原住民／托雷斯海峡岛民文化的权利。

sc.zh: 已废除 · 由 61DAA/61DAB 取代

desc.zh: 本案据 s 61DA(1) 平等共同亲职责任推定出发（仅关乎重大长期决策）。
desc.en: The case started from the s 61DA(1) presumption of equal shared parental responsibility.

oldact.zh:
61DA 作出养育令时平等共同亲职责任的推定
(1) 法院在作出关于子女的养育令时，须适用一项推定：父母对子女享有平等共同亲职责任符合子女最佳利益。【仅关乎亲职责任的分配，不涉及子女与各方相处的时间。】
(2) 若有合理理由相信父母一方（或与其同住之人）曾对子女或其他家庭成员实施虐待、或有家庭暴力，则推定不适用。
(3) 作出临时命令时，除非法院认为不宜，否则推定仍适用。
(4) 推定可被证据推翻。
oldact.en:
61DA Presumption of equal shared parental responsibility when making parenting orders
(1) When making a parenting order, the court must apply a presumption that it is in the best interests of the child for the parents to have equal shared parental responsibility. [Allocation of parental responsibility, not the time the child spends with each parent.]
(2) Does not apply if there are reasonable grounds to believe a parent (or a person living with a parent) has engaged in abuse of a child or family violence.
(3) For interim orders, the presumption applies unless the court considers it inappropriate.
(4) Rebuttable by evidence.

act.zh:
现行取代条文：
61DAA 就重大长期事项共同决策——各方须相互协商、真诚努力达成共同决定；第三方可依已传达的决定行事，无须先确认其为共同作出。
61DAB 日常决定——子女与某方相处时，就非重大长期事项，该方无须与他方协商。
act.en:
Current replacing provisions:
61DAA Joint decision-making on major long-term issues — each person must consult the others and make a genuine effort to reach a joint decision; third parties may act on a communicated decision without verifying it was joint.
61DAB Day-to-day decisions — when a child is with a person, that person need not consult on issues that are NOT major long-term issues.

court.zh: Family Court of Australia（全席上诉 Full Court）· 2007-11-22
court.en: Family Court of Australia (Full Court, on appeal) · 22 Nov 2007

summary.zh: 一宗搬迁（relocation）上诉案。核心问题：法院是否有权、以及是否适宜作出「强制」一方家长搬迁的命令（本案为责令母亲迁往悉尼）；并涉及宪法 s 92（州际迁徙自由）对此类命令的影响。上诉得直——因初审法官未审查要求母亲迁居的「合理可行性」。
summary.en: A relocation appeal. Central questions: whether the court has power, and whether it is proper, to make orders that 'coerce' a parent to relocate (here, requiring the mother to move to Sydney); and the effect of s 92 of the Constitution on such interstate-relocation orders. The appeal succeeded because the trial judge had not considered the reasonable practicability of requiring the mother to relocate.

prop.zh: 针对家长的搬迁命令具有「强制（coercive）」性质——等于迫使家长在并非其自身选择的情形下承担育养角色。此类命令只在「确有必要」时才适宜；作出前，法院须审查其「合理可行性」（如就业机会、育儿成本、对方的扶养能力等）。
prop.en: A relocation order directed at a parent is 'coercive' — it effectively forces the parent to parent in circumstances not of their choosing. Such an order is only appropriate where a coercive effect is required; and before making one the court must consider its 'reasonable practicability' (work opportunities, childcare costs, the other parent's capacity to support, etc.).

Sampson.passage:
[18] / [59]: “a relocation order directed to a parent is likely only to be appropriate where a coercive effect is required … the order will be coercive in nature and be equivalent to forcing that parent to discharge a role in circumstances not of that parent's choosing.”

[88]: “the wife's capacity to work measured against her care of the children, the costs of child care, the availability of work for the mother, the capacity of the father to meet any need of the mother for support, all … had to be addressed before an order having such a drastic and coercive effect could properly be made.”

src.zh: Mansur 判决引用：“Authorities such as Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) … the court should be careful in making orders which have a coercive effect upon a parent.” —— 用以支持「不应作出对家长有强制效果的命令」。
src.en: As cited in Mansur: “Authorities such as Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) … the court should be careful in making orders which have a coercive effect upon a parent.” — supporting that the court should not make an order with a coercive effect on a parent.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

The applicant mother, Ms Mansur (now aged 44 years), and the respondent father, Mr Mansur (now aged 46 years), are unable to agree on whether their two sons, X, born 2009, and Y, born 2013, should live with the mother in Australia in Region B or in City C, New Zealand, with the father. The Reasons which follow seek to explain why the Court has formed the view that it is in the children’s best interests at this stage that they remain living in Australia with the mother despite the father being obliged to take up a responsibility as an educator in City C from early 2024.

PRINCIPLES

In all cases involving parenting orders, the child’s best interests are the Court’s paramount consideration. In determining those interests the Court must consider not only the objects of s 60B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) and the right of a child to have a meaningful relationship with all those people significant to them, but also the primary considerations under s 60CC(2) and the additional considerations under s 60CC(3) which will be analysed below to ensure that the order I propose will serve the best interests of the children.
To the extent possible, the Court should ensure orders made do not expose a party or a child to unacceptable risk of harm through family violence, abuse or neglect.
In certain circumstances the Court applies a statutory presumption that it is in the child’s best interests for parents to have equal shared parental responsibility (s 61DA(1)), which relates to making major decisions and not about the time a child spends with each parent.
It will assist in this case to provide a context to the dispute. In doing so, statements of fact shall be construed as findings of fact.

BACKGROUND

The parties commenced cohabitation in New Zealand in 2000, initially living with the father’s parents. The father was 23 years of age and the mother was 21 years of age at that time. They married some two years later in 2003. The young couple moved to the United Kingdon in 2003 and returned to live in Australia in 2007. Australia is the birth country of the mother, whilst New Zealand is the birth country of the father.
In 2009 X was born. He is now 14 years of age and in grade 8 at D School in Region B. In 2010 the family moved to New Zealand when X was less than one year of age. They lived in and around the City C area. Y was born in New Zealand in 2013 and is now 10 years of age. He currently attends grade 5 at the E School in Region B.
In 2016 the family moved back to Region B. It was acknowledged by the father that he had prior to that date formed a relationship of some sort with Ms F. In 2017 the father returned to live in New Zealand. This was a difficult time for the family as the children were separated from the father, and the mother, in my view with some understanding of the needs of the children, chose voluntarily to return to New Zealand (the father was then living in City G) around two months later.
It is not necessary in these Reasons to deal with some of the events that occurred after the mother returned to New Zealand with the boys, save to note that in early 2018 it is acknowledged that the marriage had come to an end, and around the same time the parties entered into a parenting agreement, which is dated 31 January 2018. That parenting agreement provided that the parties had separated on 4 January 2018; that the boys were to remain in the day-to-day care of the mother in Australia; and there is the acknowledgement at clause 2 of the parenting agreement that at the time “the parties agreed that [X] and [Y] are returning to live in Australia with [Ms Mansur] because it is in their best interests to do so”; the parties committed, despite the distance, to use their best endeavours to co-parent effectively with each other. The parties agreed on extensive time arrangements, subject to being able to travel between the two countries.
This parenting agreement was executed in New Zealand. The Court does not know what effect the parenting agreement had in New Zealand, but certainly the parties appear to have complied with the terms of the agreement without significant difficulty. Between early 2018, when the mother returned to Australia with the boys, and early 2019, when the father decided to return to Region B to be closer to the family, the evidence suggests and I accept that the father and the children spent time on approximately four occasions over that nearly 12-month period.
In early 2019 the mother commenced a relationship with Mr H (aged 46 years). Mr H is the father of two children, J, now aged 14 years, and K, now aged 12 years. Those children live in a week about arrangement in Region B between Mr H and the mother of those children.
The mother commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit Court of Australia (as it was then known) on 4 June 2021. In those proceedings, the mother sought orders that the children live with her in Australia. She also sought an airport watch order. There was some cross‑examination in the trial before me about the effect of the mother seeking to obtain that order at that time. It is clear by the nature of the mother’s application that the mother was concerned that there was a prospect that the father would seek that the children live with him in New Zealand. I do not regard it as inappropriate in those circumstances, where the parties had not consented at that stage in any enforceable way in Australia to travel arrangements, for the mother to seek protection, initially, through an airport watch order. To the extent that the father said that was the catalyst for a breach in trust between him and the mother, I regard the father’s actions as somewhat overstated.
The first family report of Ms L was produced on about 16 February 2022. By this time, the mother and Mr H had commenced cohabitation (that occurring in 2020). I should indicate that, without giving particular details of every event, that these parties have done all they could to seek to resolve the seminal issue that remains to be determined, as I identified in the introduction to these reasons.
It can be shown that they engaged in at least three family reports; private mediation on more than one occasion; engaged in family therapy and; engaged the services of Ms M as a parenting coordinator. I observed, at the commencement of the trial, that the issue that has required this Court’s determination, despite all those endeavours, remains because the parties were understandably, been unable to reach a compromise. After the father moved back to Australia in early 2019, and the parties were able to transition within six months from him returning to an equal time regime by agreement, the report of Ms L persuaded the parties to consent to orders before Judge Vasta on 30 April 2022 whereby the time that the children spent with the father would be nine nights a fortnight and five nights a fortnight with the mother.
I am satisfied that the issue of the father relocating to New Zealand was an underlying issue, although, perhaps not at the forefront of the parties’ minds on a regular basis. Certainly, when the father filed an Amended Response on 2 February 2023 seeking orders that the children live with him in New Zealand, the dispute was clearly identified.
As will become obvious, in my view, these children have been aware of this ongoing issue for some years. The matter was transferred to Division 1 in accordance with the protocol between the Courts.
When the matter came first before me on 22 March 2023, by consent, the time arrangements reverted back to an equal time arrangement, week about with changeover on a Wednesday.
Non-reportable counselling was ordered and a fresh family report, engaging Mr N, an experienced social worker, was agreed to by the parents. I accept that there were some issues with counselling for Y relating to retained services of Dr P. I do not regard it as particularly necessary or helpful to deal with that particular matter at any great length, other than to observe that the mother acknowledged that she felt it was in the best interests of Y to engage in counselling; knew that she was required, where the parties had equal shared parental responsibility, to do so consensually with the father (which did not happen), and that the father felt somewhat offended by Y engaging in counselling (which on the evidence, only occurred twice, in any event) thinking he had, in some way, been minimised in his role as a co-parent of the children.
He acknowledged that his personality is such that he requires and prefers quick responses to requests for information or opinions from the other parents a timetable not always achieved during this matter, or thought quite as necessary by the mother. Nonetheless, by early 2023, with the clear issue of relocation to New Zealand on the agenda, some tensions occurred between the mother and the father on a day related to Mother’s Day 2023. The mother felt stressed and anxious about the pending the need to make a decision or to proceed with a trial about the relocation, such that she felt she was not able to function as well at work as she might have hoped. She negotiated arrangements with her employer to reduce her work from full time to part time.
On 1 August 2023, the parties had interviews with Mr N, resulting in a report dated 11 September 2023. Whilst I refer to that report later in these reasons, the final paragraph of Mr N’s report was very confronting for, at least, the mother, but I suspect for both parties, and provided as follows:
I am of an overall view the children are at greater risk of upheaval and instability if they relocate to New Zealand, partly because the mother sees herself as having no choice but also to relocate. Despite the loss it poses to the children, a scenario in which both parents maintain enduring social stability is likely to have a positive bearing on their long-term outcomes. To that end, if the father relocates to New Zealand, I recommend that the children remain in Australia in the mother’s primary care.
I was informed on 17 October 2023, the parties were unable to resolve the matter, but that it required an early trial, as the father was likely to commence employment in New Zealand in early 2024. The matter was listed for trial to commence on 13 December 2023, as it did. I pay tribute to the father who represented himself in the hearing. He is a very articulate man and whilst emotional at some times, which was understandable, he conducted the trial perfectly well and was well prepared in terms of his cross-examination and final submissions. The mother had a benefit of experienced counsel, Ms Horsley of Counsel, and instructing solicitors.
I should mention at this stage that when the matter was listed for trial, and the father was represented, I raised with the parties whether, in the circumstances, an Independent Children’s Lawyer needed to be appointed. I was aware of authorities of the Full Court suggesting that in most cases, that would be appropriate.
The parties were concerned that the appointment of an Independent Children’s Lawyer would delay a determination of the matter, which would be contrary to the best interests of the children. Accordingly, the matter was set down for hearing and determined without an independent children’s lawyer having been appointed or appearing before the court.
I made the observation at the commencement of the hearing that, in many ways, this is not a relocation case, as often known and described, but rather, a competing residence case; that is because there is no suggestion that the father will remain in Australia if the children are not able to relocate to New Zealand, as he seeks. That is because the father, as reflected in the contract of employment that was marked in the proceedings as Exhibit 4, has a contract as the educator in City C. He expects to leave Australia to take up preparation for that employment in early 2024. I should mention at this stage that although the parties were invited to indicate whether they wished to have my decision prior to Christmas 2023, both parties indicated they would prefer for the Reasons and Judgment to be delivered after Christmas so that they could, effectively, enjoy the time with the boys over Christmas without the shadow of my decision having been cast.
As is clear, the primary issue I need to decide is where the children live. Again, reflective of the quality of the parents and their desire to try to reach agreements on matters they can, the Court was provided and marked as Exhibit 1, a raft of orders that had been discussed between the parties and agreed to in three different classes, namely agreed orders; orders the mother asked the Court to make; and orders that the father seeks the Court to make, also within the various scenarios, namely:
(a) In the event that the court determines the children remain in Australia;

(b) In the event that the children are permitted to relocate to New Zealand;

(c) In the event that the children relocate to New Zealand and the mother relocates to New Zealand in the future.

When I discuss the terms of orders I have decided to make in the best interests of the children, I will return to some aspects of Exhibit 1. After the trial was completed, the Court received a slightly different Exhibit 1 and also an email from the father dated 20 December 2023, all of what I have considered, those documents have been marked as Exhibit 5.
Mr N helpfully, in his family report, identified what he described as advantages and disadvantages of the parties’ competing proposals. I incorporate in these Reasons, arising from the evaluation by Mr N:
In terms of the dispute, it appears the Court is asked to consider two possible outcomes:
The children live in [City C] with the scope to spend significant and substantial time with both parents during school terms and school holidays, as the mother states she will also move to New Zealand in those circumstances.
The children live in Australia, primarily with the mother, while the father relocates to [City C]. In this scenario, the parents contemplate the children spending the bulk of their school holidays with the father and measures for him to spend blocks of time with them in Australia during school terms, when he is on holiday.
I identify advantages and disadvantages with the father’s proposal. The advantages include:
It aligns with [X’s] views, expressed clearly and with force, and there is a greater chance of him feeling stable and happy.
It allows the children stronger connections to the paternal family members.
It allows the continuation of a structure where the children draw heavily on the support of both parents.
It is likely to create greater happiness and stability for the father, and likely to increase his capacity to provide for the children physically and emotionally.
Having a twelve-month review of the arrangement through dispute resolution or an updated family report allows the children the scope to express views about their situation from a more informed position.
175. The disadvantages include:
It causes significant structural change and upheaval for the children. It requires them to change schools and sever peer connections.
It creates a significant upheaval for the mother, to separate from her long-term partner and maintain independent accommodation in another country, against her wishes, and to find secure employment and become established in a different community.
The father’s proposed living and working arrangement is untested.
Having a formal review of the children’s arrangement after twelve months risks further pressure being placed on the children by the parents, to their detriment.
I identify advantages and disadvantages with the mother’s proposal for the children to remain in Australia, with the father likely to live in New Zealand. The advantages include:
It allows the children to maintain their daily lives in the same school communities.
Both parents are likely to have greater social integration and stability under this scenario.
It allows the children to enjoy family life with each parent, within their respective communities.
The children are of an age developmentally where they can maintain meaningful relationships with the parents from a distance through communication and more spaced out, more intense blocks of time.
Having the children go to and from school from one home rather than two allows more consistency during the week. It reduces the extent to which the parents must communicate about daily matters.

177. The disadvantages include:
It is likely to pose a degree of loss and thus distress to the children, particularly in the medium term, at their separation from their father.
It goes against [X’s] sincerely expressed views and he may struggle with this situation over time, particularly when managing transitions back to the mother at the end of holiday time. I see potential for ongoing conflict and disruption in managing this arrangement.
Practical difficulties may arise given the difference in school holiday dates between the jurisdictions.
The children have had some previous difficulty coping in the mother’s blended family household. It brings the larger sibling group together for longer blocks of time, which adds pressure to these dynamics.
This is a difficult and finely balanced matter. If the children end up living at a prohibitive distance from the mother or father, I suggest the following measures are installed to enable them to maintain a meaningful relationship with the ‘distant’ parent:
a. Electronic communication at reasonable times.
The scope to spend a week at a time with the distant parent on at least one occasion during each school term, if the distant parent can remain within practical distance of the children’s school/s.
A leaning of school holiday time toward the distant parent so the children may travel to their home. I suggest all the Autumn and Spring holidays, 10 days of the winter holiday and half of the summer holiday. I suggest the summer holiday is rotated so the children alternate Christmas Day in each home.
Ongoing authorities for both parents to communicate about the children with their school/s and service providers.
Ongoing obligations on the parents to inform each other of all the children's health, educational and developmental concerns, including to consult about their extra-curricular activities.
As the father, in his effective cross-examination of Mr N raised with Mr N, many of the advantages and disadvantages are common to both scenarios. As has been mentioned on more than one occasion during the case management of this case and the hearing, it is not in any way optimal for these two parents to be living many thousands of kilometres away from each other.
The fact that the costs of travel between Region B and City C (and other parts of New Zealand) can be managed both frequently and fairly cost effectively (and acknowledged by the parents with the children flying unaccompanied) only goes some short way to making the clearly less optimal arrangements manageable. Nonetheless, it is still far from ideal. I will refer, later in these Reasons, to the primary and additional considerations, being the legislative pathway identified by the legislation in determining what is in the best interests of the children. Because of the remarks I will make in this Judgment in relation to the next issues, I will undertake that analysis in a narrative form.

THE MOTHER’S HOUSEHOLD

The mother, by her application initiating these proceedings, says and has continued to say that it is in the best interests of the children that the children live with her in Australia when the father moves back to New Zealand. I find on the evidence, at least, the following matters of relevance.
The mother is well aware of the strong wishes to relocate expressed by the oldest child, X. As I will mention later in these reasons, I am satisfied that they are his genuine and strongly held wishes. Whilst the mother has expressed difficulty during the course of these proceedings, and even in the witness box, to accept that the wishes could be expressed so strongly by X without significant influence by the father, I am not satisfied that that has been the case.
The wishes expressed by X identify that he feels his father “gets him”. It seems to me that the acknowledgement of that close attuned emotional relationship between X and the father has been a difficult issue for the mother to accept. She gave evidence that she and X are able to speak about a range of topics openly and honestly, but not this issue of relocation. Part of the reason why, she says, she has some concerns that the wishes are in fact his wishes, genuinely held, is that X has not been able to speak to her about it. She did acknowledge, in cross‑examination, and also some questions raised by the bench, that X may find it difficult to raise the issue with her when he is likely to know that the mother’s position is contrary to his wishes.
Nonetheless, and save for this issue, there are no areas of concern in the relationship between the mother and X. She has been, at times, his exclusive carer when the father, as the history shows, elected to remain in New Zealand when the children returned to Australia, although, that was some years ago.
Y is a younger child with a quite different personality. His wishes and feelings expressed to Mr N suggest that he is likely to miss his mother if he lives in New Zealand more than he is likely to miss his father if he lives in Australia and his father lives in New Zealand. Again, the differentiation is narrow and not, of itself, determinative, but reflects, in my view, the history of care, the age of the child, and the parenting role the mother has undertaken with Y, which does seem to be closer, in some respects, at this stage than it is with X.
Having had the benefit of observing the mother and Mr H in the witness box, I am satisfied, as Mr N opines, that they have a solid and effective relationship. It is a relationship that has been tested at times, not always completely happily, when the five children (the subject boys and the three children of Mr H) cohabit in the same home. I accept there have been tensions, and at one stage, the mother’s parents, who reside in Region B and support the mother (although they provided no evidence in this trial), offered a bit of pressure release every alternate Friday, and in fact, were happy, at times, to increase the amount of time they spent with their grandsons so as to allow Mr H to spend “one on one time” with his children when they were in his care. To the extent that this was being suggested by the father to be a criticism, I do not agree.
One of the issues which I accept, is that the grandparents have an intact and ongoing relationship with their grandsons, which on all the evidence, their grandsons enjoy.
It was submitted by Ms Horsley on behalf of the mother that the court would find that the mother is better placed to meet the emotional needs of the children through what is, as everybody acknowledges, likely to be a very difficult time for the children with the parents not only separated, but unlike in the past, seemingly separated in different countries on a permanent basis.
I am satisfied that the mother is likely to return to work, although as the female role model during the time the children and Mr H’s children are in the home together, she will have duties relating to her employment as a public servant and I have no concerns about Mr H and the mother working as a team to meet that range of responsibilities that include day to day care, attending school, pursuing peer relationships and extracurricular activities. Again, I say that in my view, the relationship between the mother and Mr H has not only been tested by the tensions arising from these ongoing proceedings, but on my assessment, and I find they have passed that test.

THE FATHER’S HOUSEHOLD

One of the issues raised on the evidence and, it seems, one of the “tipping points” described by Mr N in his cross-examination, is the untested nature of the father’s household that involves himself, Ms F, Ms F’s children, Q (aged 18) and R (aged 14), and on the father’s proposals, the two boys that are the subject of these proceedings. The father, I sensed, felt unfairly criticised by the descriptor of his relationship with Ms F as “untested”. However, he acknowledged that the amounts of time he and Ms F and the children have spent together as a family unit would be no more than four or five weeks a year. That has sometimes been in New Zealand and occasionally in Australia. I had the benefit of seeing Ms F in the witness box.
She impressed, much as Mr H did, as supportive of her partner and willing to do what was necessary to make the children happy. No criticisms of her as a person were or could be advanced. To the extent that to some degree some submissions were made about the poor communication between the mother and Ms F, in my view, that is more associated with the nature of these proceedings, and the uncertainty of these proceedings are not reflected of the personalities of these two quite impressive women. The father’s household in New Zealand is untested. If the children relocate, they will be in a home that involves two children (although Q may not be there as often) with whom they have a relationship but not a long relationship. Both boys have expressed to Mr N how comfortable they feel with Ms F and that is attribute to the way in which he has developed a relationship with them. There is no sense that she has, in any way, sought to introduce herself as an alternate mother.
Although, to be fair, if the children do live primarily in New Zealand as the father proposes, that is a role that she will be, to some degree undertaking, namely the predominant day-to-day female role model in the home of the children. I find, as might be expected, that the father’s desire to return to his homeland has meant that he has, to some degree, minimised some of the challenges of adjusting back to living in New Zealand for the children. It is true that the children have lived in New Zealand, but always with their parents, never, as will be their position now, with one parent being in Australia.
As I will refer to later in these reasons, X strongly identifies as a New Zealander. He has made comments which have been repeated by others that that is his “home”. However, it is my view that there will be tensions that will need to be worked through if the children live in New Zealand with the father, Ms F and her two children, which have not been tested and include the father not only adjusting to a new employment and the stresses of that employment but also to actually living day in, day out with Ms F. I have no doubt that Ms F and the father are committed to their relationship. The fact that their relationship may have even commenced in some way prior to separation and has endured what is now a period of nearly six years where they are living in different countries primarily is a tribute to their commitment to each other. Nonetheless, their commitment at an adult level needs to be replicated in the household when other stressors are being exerted upon them and the children.
Some criticism was made of the father about what level of financial commitment he might continue to offer. In my view, both the mother and father will have the capacity to support their children to some extent, wherever they live, and I believe they will do so. The orders that will be made in relation to travel between the two countries being shared by the parents is the most clear demonstration of their commitment.
The father, in terms of his proposal, draws strongly on satisfying the genuine express wishes of his son, X. He was entitled to do so. The evidence reveals that the wishes have been expressed consistently for some time. The father indicated that he would “not be here” but for the wishes expressed by X in particular (and to a lesser extent, by Y). By that, I took it to mean that if X had expressed to him a desire to remain living in Australia, he would not have expended the time, effort (and when he had lawyers – money) to pursue orders of the Court, opposed by the mother, that the children live with him in New Zealand.
It may be that the father needs to be able to express to X at some stage that he fought for what X wanted. I understand why that might be his perspective. In saying that, however, the father, in his evidence, genuinely believes the children’s best interests will be served by living with him in New Zealand where he says, and I accept, he feels he will be happier. There is nothing novel about the suggestion that a happy parent usually makes a better parent. The mother who has now been living in Australia after periods of time living in New Zealand and elsewhere and where she has formed an enduring relationship, has security of employment and likes the area she lives. The father craves to return to his country of origin. It is not an unreasonable pull on him emotionally to do so. Not only is New Zealand an environment in which he is familiar, but he has family support there.
The grandparents in New Zealand are no less important that the grandparents in Australia to these boys. There is evidence that the boys enjoy their time with their New Zealand grandparents, although, of course, over the last few years that have had less opportunity to spend time with them than they have with the maternal grandparents. Overall, the father can only see positives about the move to New Zealand by which I mean that he believes any of the concerns associated with the distress to children might feel, from being separated from the mother, he can manage empathetically, sympathetically and, if necessary, with therapeutic support. I had no concerns that he would seek to do so.
Before considering the competing proposals within the matrix of the primary and additional considerations, I make the observation that Mr N opined, and I find, that X and Y are, indeed, fortunate children. They are greatly loved by their mother, their father and their respective extended families. They are genuinely loved by their parents’ new partners. Their life is likely to be enriched by engagement with the children of their parents’ new partners. The mother and father’s case are not required to meet the challenges which so often affect parenting capacity, such as significant mental health issues, substance and alcohol abuse, family violence, and the like. In fact, it is comforting to see, although both mother and father have been tested at times, how they have each tried to avoid involving the children in this dispute. Although, it is said that the father may have, by his personality or otherwise, be more inclined to involve the children than the mother, the best testament to the way these parents have worked is how they have managed a shared care regime with very few difficulties for now, nearly, six years since separation.
That would have, as a matter of common sense, imbibed many occasions where compromise; turning the other cheek and merely moving on would have been reactions to an action or reaction by the other parent.

PRIMARY CONSIDERATIONS

When the parties lived in separate countries, it is inevitable that even if there is a benefit to the children having a relationship with the parent, as there is in this case, the nature of being able to fulfil that relationship will be very different from that which exists when they live, as they do now in adjoining suburbs. Nonetheless, I find that it is to the benefit of the children that they have a meaningful relationship with each parent.
As earlier indicated, section 60CC(2)(b) requires the Court to consider “the need to protect the child from physical or psychological harm from being subjected to, or exposed to, abuse, neglect or family violence.” In my view, thankfully for these boys, these issues do not loom large in this case. The conflict between the parents has been contained as much as possible, however, the children are aware that we have reached the pinnacle of the dispute. In my view, it is an ongoing emotional risk to the children if a decision is not made. It is my view that the mother’s alternate proposal that if relocation was permitted, it should be delayed, would not be in the best interests of the children and likely to expose the children to more psychological harm of uncertainty, at least. Even if there was an order made that the children were to relocate, for example, to New Zealand in 12 months’ time as the mother proposes (mostly, she would say, based on the need to allow the father to get ready for the children to come and settle into his new household and new job, and for the children to complete some of their schooling in Australia), the Court can offer no guarantee or any certainty that if the mother found that the children were even more settled than she currently says they are, and it would be contrary to their interests to move in 12 months’ time to New Zealand (or, for example, if X, for some reason, changed his wishes), then further litigation relating to the move to New Zealand could start again.
I am firmly of the view, and Mr N agreed with this proposition that a decision needs to be made and the parents need to do their best to support the decision once made. By so doing, the children are better able to cope with the consequences of a difficult decision which has, as Mr N has already identified, positives and negatives.

ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS

I am satisfied that X genuinely expresses a desire to live with the father in New Zealand, once the father moves.
The attraction, it seems to me, on the evidence, is that X strongly identifies with New Zealand and with the father now having decided to move back to his country of origin, this opens up the opportunity that has not otherwise been available until the father took the job in City C to return to live in New Zealand. In my view, at least as much as living with the father is the child’s desire to live in New Zealand, a country with which he identifies. This has been a constant wish expressed on more than one occasion. It must be given weight, but in my view, as I would explain, there are other factors which need to be taken into consideration in determining what is in the best interests of both children.
Y is more flexible. My sense from what he has expressed to Mr N, is that he acknowledges it would be a great loss for him to be absent from the day to day care of his mother. He loves his father and he loves his big brother, and my sense is that he will cope with whatever decision is made more comfortably based on his wishes, maturity and age of development, than X is likely to be able to achieve.
I have already indicated that these children have strong relationships with each of their parents, and their parents’ partners and extended family. I would not regard there to be such a significant differentiation in a form and strength of these relationships to be factor likely to assist in this case.
The parents have participated in making decisions (save for the Dr P issue and a few other examples) and as I say, have maintained a shared care arrangement for nearly six years. At the conclusion of these Reasons I will deal with section 60CC(3)(d) namely “the likely effect of any changes in the child's circumstances”. The mother and father are both likely to have sufficient income to be able to afford as they say they will, an equal contribution to the travel expenses between Australia and New Zealand, wherever the children live. The parents accept that in between physical visits, access to all forms of electronic communication between the children and the non-resident parent is important.
I did not discern on the evidence, nor do I find any significant difference in the capacity or attitude to parenting of the parents. In fact, they do, on the evidence, parent quite similarly, although one might expect there are nuanced differences all within appropriate parenting range. Values of hard work, getting a good education, getting on with others and being respectful are, it seems to me, core values that are reflected in each household.
There are no family violence orders in existence.
The court is required by section 60CC(3) to consider whether it would be preferable to make the order that would be least likely to lead to the institution of further proceedings in relation to the children. In my view, on the evidence I would find that making a decision effective now rather than delaying it, as is the mother’s proposition to invite revisiting in 12 months’ time the same issue.
These children have had this uncertainty hanging over their head for many years, although not actually materialised in a manifest way until more recently. Nonetheless, they need certainty, and, in my view, that is the best way to relieve the prospect of the institution of further proceedings, save for one aspect which I will refer to shortly.

PARENTAL RESPONSIBILITY

Both parents accept that it is in the best interest of the children that they continue to equally share parental responsibility. The enforceability of such an order in New Zealand is a matter to which there has been no domestic law evidence given, but which I am satisfied is able to be achieved by the conduct of the parties as it has in the past.

WHERE SHOULD THE CHILDREN LIVE?

Ms Horsley submitted that the court would find that when the father committed to moving to New Zealand for the quite understandable reasons that he has expressed and which I accept relate to reconnecting with his family, his country of origin and his partner, Ms F, that he did so knowing that there was a prospect that the children would not be permitted by the Court to relocate with him. She says and I accept that this means the father, at some level, should be seen to understand and concede that the mother will be able to meet the needs of the children in Australia if he lives in New Zealand. I accept that submission.
The mother says that if the children are permitted to relocate, then she would certainly consider and likely would move to New Zealand. It is not her preference. It would mean, most likely, the cessation of a long relationship she has now developed with her partner, Mr H. It would mean she would need to find employment in a country in which I infer she has been employed before but does not have the same level of connection that she has developed since living in Australia since, at least, 2018 when she returned from New Zealand.
She, of course, has family support in Region B that she does not have from her own family to the same level in New Zealand. While she acknowledged she has some “great girlfriends” in New Zealand, moving back to that country does not necessarily mean that their relationships will be maintained to the same level as they may have been many years ago. She is prepared to consider moving back to New Zealand if the children live there, which would involve also a sale of a home she has purchased.
Authorities such as Sampson & Hartnett (No 10) [2007] FamCA 1365; (2007) 38 Fam LR 315 make it clear that the court should be careful in making orders which have a coercive effect upon a parent. If the children live in New Zealand, the mother may ultimately decide to return or she may not.
One of the significant factors in this case which has troubled the court in its determination and one over which the court has spent some time in consideration is the effect, if the children do not relocate to New Zealand, of X’s strongly held views not being supported.
When the Court, through its processes, invites children to express a view through a family report writer or otherwise or whether as a result of maturity, they are keen to be heard, there are difficulties that arise when having expressed their views, they are not supported in relation to the future possible management of the disappointment and/or distress or sense of not being heard that a child of the age of X might feel. It is, therefore, in many ways appropriate, when a strongly expressed view is evidenced by a mature-age child, to support those views.
However, I am satisfied that although I do not expect X’s disappointment to be easily managed, supporting his views will have a consequent effect upon the relationship with his brother, Y, and Y’s happiness which I find is shaped by being with his brother and being in the home of his mother. I could not discount the prospect that X might, if required to remain in Australia, act out. I am satisfied that the mother is well alert to such a possibility. So much is obvious from paragraph 303 of her affidavit where she says as follows:
I have discussed at length with [Mr H] the prospect of [X] (or, in fact, [Y]) being older and expressing a desire to move to New Zealand. I accept, that if the boys stay in Australia if [X] expresses a wish when he is older, to relocate to New Zealand, and we can determine that it is indeed a genuine wish, and that it is in [X’s] best interests to move then I will very serious consideration to that happening. It is really hard to put into words an age, or a specific point when that may be, as we had no idea how the boys will or will not flourish if [Mr Mansur] moves to New Zealand and they are without the stress of a relocation or of a split household. All I can say is that I have always done what is in the boy’s best interest, and I will continue to do that as a good parent. For now, though, I have serious concerns about whether [X] is expressing a genuine wish and whether he truly understands the ramification of such a move.
(as per original)
I regard that expression, although, no doubt, drawn by her experienced Family Law expert lawyer but adopted by the mother, as giving me comfort that she understands that a point may arise where she needs to consider in the future X moving to New Zealand.

CONCLUSION

This is, as everyone has described, a finely balanced matter. Probably, the boys will cope if required to live in New Zealand with the father or with the mother in Australia in time. It is very difficult to differentiate between the proposals but, in my view, I have done so by drawing on the two factors which I think are the most important differentiation, namely:
(a) what weight can be applied to X’s wishes and the effect of that on his relationship with his brother and including the concerns that arise from not following his wishes as expressed; and

(b) the uncertainties of these children, who have lived in Australia with their parents, moving to New Zealand into a household arrangement; new schools; new dynamics with other children which is quite untested.

For the Reasons expressed, I have formed the view that the children should remain living in Australia with the mother. The mother will have to confront the disappointment, sadness and perhaps even anger of X. To perhaps, at least, assist the children getting an independent appraisal of why the court has made this decision in the way that it has, the parties agree that Dr S, who has been engaged with non-reportable counselling, then be engaged by the parents at their joint cost to explain, as best as possible in language the children understand, and, if not accept, at least, appreciate, why the Court has made the decision it has.
The mother will need to deal with the fallout. Dealing with the fallout will be better achieved for X if the father’s emotional reaction and disappointment in the decision is, at the very least, camouflaged from the children. Having seen the father in the witness box where he, at times, became emotional and even when he was presenting his case, that will not be easy for him. However, he has done exactly what any loving parent would do. He has articulated a strong case consistent with his oldest son’s wishes and adopting a position that he believes is in the best interests of both children and pursued with all vigour and reasonable endeavour a decision from a court which is ultimately empowered to make the decision. His son, X, could hardly be disappointed with the actions of the father, because the Court has not agreed with the father.

FORM OF ORDER

In circumstances where the Court has decided that X and Y will remain living in Australia when the father relocates to New Zealand, I incorporate in the orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons, those orders agreed to by the parents.
As is apparent, it is not necessary to make alternate orders on the basis of the children relocating to New Zealand. Importantly, the orders comprehensively set out how the children will spend time with the father in New Zealand, and how travel will be facilitated.
The father in his email correspondence of 20 December 2023, raised the following issues:
(a) Although it is acknowledged that the school holidays in Australia the children will be entitled to enjoy and the school term/holidays which the father (working in New Zealand) will not always be the same, a great deal of effort deems to have been exerted to try and maximise (in the agreed ordered) the opportunities for the children to spend time with the father. I have included, at paragraph 5, the simple phrase “unless otherwise agreed between the parents in writing” – so as to remind the parents that they can deviate from the orders if they agree to do so, in particular circumstances, because it is in the children’s best interests. They are not required to return to a Court if they agree on the variation. These are sensible parents who have shown a capacity at times to be flexible. As a result, whilst consistent with the father’s desire for absolute literal certainty, some need for flexibility is important.

(b) The father seeks a variation to paragraph 6.7 to allow the children to spend up to ten nights with him, ideally the “cross-over nights”. I agree with that variation.

(c) I see no need to incorporate in paragraphs 6.9 and 6.10 a certain 21 day period as the father proposes. The orders allow, in effect, for Christmas to be spent with the mother for Christmas 2024 and each even year thereafter and with the father for Christmas 2025 and each odd year thereafter. As already indicated, if the father wishes to alter the commencement date, he can do so, but the end date will be fixed. If because of circumstances the father, in a year, has less than 21 days with the boys, then that should be the case, rather than requesting the mother to adjust her arrangements (including work obligations), unless she otherwise agrees in writing.

(d) The father says that, a time needs to be set for notification to the other parent about the airfares book by the parent, pursuant to paragraph 8.6. I have amended proposed orders 8 to simplify the process, whilst acknowledging that:

(i) It is likely to be cost effective for flights to be booked early; and

(ii) The parents are well aware of the distances between the airport and where they live, and such are to be sensible about nominating times for departure that do not require unreasonably early departure times or late arrival times. Seeking to prescribe the exercise of “common sense” is never easy, and for these parents in my view, unnecessary.

(e) I regard proposed paragraph 19 as too prescriptive, and capable of leading to further proceedings by way of enforcement. With the regular telephone communication ordered, and with the obligations to keep each parent informed, I see no need to prescribe the “weekly update” proposed.

(f) The proposals for additional overseas travel should, considering the travel already prescribed, not occur without at least two months written notice.

(g) With the parents likely now to be living in two different countries, I am not persuaded that it is necessary, or even practically achievable, to engage the services of a parenting co-ordinator. Both Australia and New Zealand legislation prescribe “pre-action” procedures. These parents have shown capacity to engage in multiple dispute resolution events and I see no need to prescribe any particular practitioner.

(h) The parents agree that Dr S should explain these orders to the children. As to any need, now this matter has been determined, for ongoing therapeutic counselling, I am less than satisfied about that benefit. I had no evidence from Dr S but am confident the mother is alert to the possible benefits that could flow from the adjustment to this decision by both boys, and if Dr S is available and happy to assist, then I am comfortable the mother will make whatever arrangements are appropriate.

Finally, it is not clear whether either party wishes to pursue any application for costs. Whilst not either encouraging (or discouraging) any such application, the Rules provide how any such application is to be provided. It would be my usual practice, for issues of costs, to be determined by written submissions.
I find that the orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons, are in the best interests of both children at this time.
